Understanding DTI Ratios

Debt-to-income ratio (DTI) measures how much of your gross monthly income is consumed by debt payments. Lenders use two versions of this ratio: the front-end ratio and the back-end ratio.

Front-end DTI (also called the housing ratio) measures only your proposed new housing payment — principal, interest, taxes, insurance, and HOA fees if applicable — as a percentage of your gross monthly income. The traditional guideline is 28 percent or below, meaning your housing payment should not exceed 28 percent of gross income. In practice, many loan programs and lenders allow higher front-end ratios for strong borrowers.

Back-end DTI measures all monthly debt obligations — housing payment plus car loans, student loans, credit card minimum payments, child support, and any other recurring debts — as a percentage of gross monthly income. The traditional guideline is 36 percent or below (the "28/36 rule"), but many loan programs allow considerably higher ratios.

For conventional loans, the typical maximum back-end DTI is 45 percent, and AUS systems can approve up to 50 percent for strong borrowers with compensating factors like excellent credit or significant reserves. For FHA loans, back-end DTI up to 57 percent can be approved by AUS for qualified borrowers. For VA loans, there is technically no maximum DTI, though lenders typically target under 41 percent and require a residual income analysis for borrowers above that threshold.

High DTI Warning: A high back-end DTI — even if technically within program guidelines — can limit your options with certain lenders and increase your interest rate. If you are close to DTI limits, paying down revolving debt (credit card balances) before applying for pre-approval is one of the fastest ways to improve your position.

What Affects Your Pre-Approval Amount

Your pre-approval amount reflects the maximum loan a lender is willing to extend based on your financial profile. The key variables are:

Gross Income: Lenders use gross (pre-tax) income — not net take-home pay — to calculate DTI. Overtime, bonus, and commission income can be used if it has been earned consistently for at least two years and is likely to continue.

Monthly Debt Obligations: Every recurring monthly debt payment reduces the room available in your DTI for a housing payment. A $600 car payment, for example, directly reduces the maximum mortgage payment you can qualify for by that same $600 from a DTI standpoint.

Credit Score: A higher credit score unlocks better interest rates, which means a lower monthly payment on the same loan amount — which in turn means you can qualify for a larger loan at the same income level.

Down Payment: A larger down payment reduces the loan amount needed, can eliminate PMI, and reduces your monthly payment. It can also be the deciding factor in whether a jumbo lender will approve your file.

Loan Type and Term: A 30-year term produces a lower monthly payment than a 15-year term on the same loan amount, potentially allowing you to qualify for a higher loan. ARM (adjustable-rate mortgage) products may qualify at the initial rate or a calculated stress-test rate depending on the lender and program.

How Long Is a Pre-Approval Valid?

Most mortgage pre-approval letters are valid for 60 to 90 days. After that period, the lender will need to refresh your credit report and verify that your financial situation has not changed. Some lenders issue 30-day letters by default; others issue 120-day letters for highly qualified borrowers. If your pre-approval is expiring and you have not yet found a home, contact your loan officer for a refresh — typically this requires updated pay stubs and bank statements rather than a full re-application.

Note that your pre-approval letter should ideally list a loan amount equal to or slightly above your target purchase price — not your absolute maximum. Sellers and listing agents do not need to see your ceiling, and a letter showing the exact price of their listing can inadvertently signal that you are at your limit.

Shopping Multiple Lenders

One of the most common fears buyers have is that shopping multiple lenders will damage their credit score. In reality, the credit scoring models used for mortgage underwriting — FICO Score 2, 4, and 5, which are the mortgage-specific versions — treat all mortgage hard inquiries within a 14-to-45-day window as a single inquiry for rate-shopping purposes. This means you can apply with three or four lenders within a two-week period and have the credit impact of a single inquiry.

Shopping multiple lenders is genuinely important. Interest rates and lender fees can vary meaningfully — even a 0.25 percent rate difference on a $400,000 loan represents thousands of dollars over the life of the loan. Closing costs also vary significantly: origination fees, discount points, title services, and prepaid items can differ by $2,000–$5,000 or more between lenders. Ask each lender for a Loan Estimate (the standardized three-page disclosure document) so you can make apples-to-apples comparisons.

Local vs. Online Lenders

Both local and online lenders have real advantages. Online lenders (Rocket Mortgage, Better, LoanDepot, etc.) often offer competitive rates, streamlined digital applications, and extended availability. They can be efficient for borrowers with straightforward W-2 income and standard documentation.

Local and regional lenders — community banks, credit unions, and local mortgage brokers — often have advantages that matter specifically in Tampa Bay: relationships with local listing agents and closing attorneys, greater flexibility for non-standard income situations (self-employed, rental income, commission-heavy earners), in-house underwriting for faster turnaround, and a named loan officer whose cell phone a listing agent can actually call. In a multiple-offer situation, a listing agent who can get a live loan officer on the phone to verify a pre-approval is meaningfully more comfortable recommending acceptance of that offer.

Mortgage brokers occupy a middle ground: they shop your file to multiple wholesale lenders, can often find niche programs (bank statement loans, asset depletion loans, DSCR loans for investors), and can be the best option for complex income situations. They are paid by the lender rather than directly by you, though their compensation is built into the loan pricing.

What to Avoid After Pre-Approval

The period between pre-approval and closing is one of the most dangerous for buyers who do not understand that lenders re-verify your financial profile immediately before funding. Many real estate transactions have fallen apart days before closing because of a change in the buyer's credit or financial situation. Here is what to absolutely avoid:

Do not open new credit accounts. A new credit card, car loan, personal loan, or buy-now-pay-later account will appear on your credit report, potentially lower your credit score, and add to your monthly debt obligations — changing your DTI. Even a store card opened "to save 10 percent" can cause problems.

Do not make large purchases on existing credit. Charging a new appliance set, vacation, or furniture to your credit cards before closing increases your utilization rate and can lower your credit score. Buy everything for the new house after closing.

Do not change jobs. A job change — even a lateral move or promotion — can require the lender to restart the employment verification process. A move from W-2 employment to self-employment is particularly problematic and can potentially disqualify a buyer mid-transaction. If a job change is unavoidable, notify your loan officer immediately.

Do not move large sums of money between accounts. Lenders track deposits and transfers. Moving money between accounts without a paper trail creates documentation headaches. If you need to consolidate funds for closing, talk to your loan officer first.

Do not co-sign on any loan. Co-signing adds that loan's payment to your monthly debt obligations from an underwriting perspective, even if you never make a payment.

Critical Reminder: Lenders pull your credit a second time — sometimes 24–48 hours before closing — to confirm nothing has changed. A new car loan, a missed payment, or an unexpected inquiry can trigger a last-minute review that delays or kills the transaction. Stay boring between pre-approval and closing.

Getting Pre-Approved for New Construction in Tampa Bay

Buying a new construction home in Tampa Bay — with builders like Pulte, Toll Brothers, DR Horton, Lennar, Taylor Morrison, and David Weekley — involves a slightly different financing dynamic. Most builders have preferred or in-house lenders and offer significant incentives — closing cost credits, rate buydowns, design center upgrades — for using their preferred lender. These incentives can be genuinely valuable; a builder's rate buydown can be worth $10,000 to $30,000 or more on higher-priced homes.

However, builder lenders do not always offer the most competitive rates outside of the incentive packages, and buyers can sometimes do better by financing independently and negotiating a comparable incentive. The key is to get a competing quote from an outside lender and compare the total package — rate, closing costs, and incentive — rather than just the headline interest rate.

For new construction, note that your pre-approval will need to be refreshed at the time of closing, which may be 6–18 months after signing the purchase contract. Interest rates, your financial situation, and lending standards can all change in that window. Discuss rate lock options with your builder's lender and any outside lender early in the process.

Pre-Approval and Contingencies in Your Offer

In a standard Florida Residential Purchase Agreement (the FAR/BAR AS IS contract most commonly used in Tampa Bay transactions), the financing contingency specifies the loan amount, loan type, interest rate ceiling, and number of days the buyer has to secure financing. If the buyer cannot obtain financing within the contingency period on the stated terms, they can cancel the contract and receive their deposit back.

Sellers in competitive markets often push for shorter financing contingency periods — 21 days instead of the standard 30 days, or even shorter. Having a strong pre-approval (particularly a full underwriting approval) means you can realistically commit to a shorter contingency period, which makes your offer more attractive without waiving the contingency entirely.

Some buyers in highly competitive situations waive the financing contingency entirely. This is a significant risk — if your financing falls through for any reason, you lose your deposit (typically 1–3 percent of the purchase price in Tampa Bay transactions). Only waive a financing contingency if you have a full underwriting approval, a thorough understanding of the property, and ideally the financial reserves to close in cash if needed.

Loan Validity
Pre-approval: 60–90 days
Rate lock: 30–60 days
CTC typical: 30–45 days
Refresh: updated docs
Rate Shopping Window
FICO treats all mortgage inquiries
within 14–45 days as one
Shop 3–4 lenders
Get Loan Estimates to compare
New Construction Note
Builder incentives: significant
Compare full package
Rate lock options vary
Refresh at closing
Contingency Tips
Shorter = stronger offer
Full UW = more flexibility
Never waive without reserves
Standard: 30 days in FL

Frequently Asked Questions: Mortgage Pre-Approval Florida

Q: How long does mortgage pre-approval take in Florida?

With a complete document package, most lenders can issue a pre-approval within 24 to 72 business hours. Some local lenders with in-house underwriting can turn same-day approvals for straightforward files. The most common delay is incomplete documentation — missing tax return pages, unexplained deposits, or incorrect income figures. Having your complete document package ready before you apply is the best way to accelerate the process.

Q: Does getting pre-approved hurt my credit score?

A mortgage pre-approval requires a hard credit inquiry, which typically reduces your FICO score by a few points (usually 2–5 points). However, because mortgage inquiries are treated as rate shopping, multiple mortgage-related hard pulls within a 14-to-45-day window count as a single inquiry. The impact is temporary and typically recovers within a few months. The benefit of having a solid pre-approval before shopping far outweighs the minor score impact.

Q: Can I get pre-approved if I am self-employed?

Yes, but the process is more involved. Self-employed borrowers need two years of complete personal and business tax returns, a year-to-date profit and loss statement, and documentation of ongoing business operation. Lenders use a two-year average of net income from tax returns, which means significant deductions that reduce taxable income also reduce qualifying income. Some self-employed buyers find bank statement loan programs — which use 12 or 24 months of bank deposits instead of tax returns — to be a better fit, though these typically carry higher rates.

Q: What is the minimum down payment for a home in Tampa Bay?

The minimum down payment depends on the loan type. Conventional loans allow as little as 3 percent down for first-time buyers. FHA requires 3.5 percent with a 580+ score. VA and USDA loans offer zero down payment for eligible borrowers. Note that any down payment below 20 percent on a conventional loan triggers PMI, which adds to your monthly cost. In Tampa Bay's median price range, even small differences in down payment percentage represent significant dollar amounts.

Q: My credit score is 580. Can I still buy a home in Florida?

Yes. An FHA loan is the most common path for buyers with scores in the 580–619 range, provided you have the 3.5 percent down payment and meet income/DTI requirements. You will pay both an upfront MIP and ongoing annual MIP, but FHA rates are often competitive with conventional rates for borrowers in this credit tier. It is also worth spending 3–6 months actively improving your score — paying down credit card balances and disputing any errors — as moving from 580 to 620 or above opens significantly more loan options and better pricing.

Q: I am buying with my spouse. Do we both need to be on the application?

You are not required to have both spouses on a mortgage application, and sometimes it makes sense to apply individually — for example, if one spouse has a significantly lower credit score that would drag down the qualifying rate. However, in Florida as a community property-adjacent state (Florida is not strictly community property, but marital home laws apply), both spouses typically need to sign certain documents at closing regardless of whose name is on the loan. Consult with your lender and a real estate attorney about the best approach for your specific situation.

Q: What are closing costs in Florida and how do I prepare for them?

Florida closing costs for buyers typically run 2–4 percent of the purchase price on top of the down payment. They include lender origination fees, appraisal, title insurance (both lender and owner policies — in Florida, the seller traditionally pays for the owner's title policy, though this is negotiable), document stamps on the mortgage (Florida charges $0.35 per $100 of mortgage), prepaid homeowners insurance, prepaid property taxes, and escrow setup. Always ask your lender for a Loan Estimate within three business days of application, which will itemize all projected closing costs.

Q: How does a VA loan work for Tampa Bay buyers near MacDill AFB?

VA loans are one of the best mortgage products available for eligible veterans and active-duty service members. They require no down payment, charge no PMI, and are available for primary residences. The VA funding fee (which replaces PMI) is typically 2.15 percent of the loan amount for first use, though it can be rolled into the loan and is waived for veterans with a service-connected disability rating. Tampa Bay's lender community is experienced with VA loans given the large military population, and working with a VA-experienced lender is strongly recommended.

Q: Can I use gift funds for my down payment?

Yes, gift funds are allowed on most loan programs, but the rules vary. For conventional loans, gifts are allowed from family members with a signed gift letter stating no repayment is required. FHA allows gifts from family, employers, and certain organizations. VA allows gifts from any source. The key documentation requirements are a gift letter, evidence the donor had the funds, and a clear paper trail showing the transfer. Large undocumented cash deposits — even legitimate gifts — can cause significant problems in underwriting.

Q: Should I choose a 15-year or 30-year mortgage?

A 15-year mortgage carries a lower interest rate (typically 0.5–0.75 percent lower) and builds equity much faster, but the monthly payment is significantly higher — often 40–50 percent higher than a 30-year payment on the same loan amount. Most buyers in Tampa Bay choose the 30-year term for the lower mandatory payment and use the flexibility to make additional principal payments when their budget allows. The right answer depends entirely on your income stability, other financial priorities, and risk tolerance. Your loan officer can model both scenarios in detail.

Q: What is a rate buydown and should I consider one in 2026?

A rate buydown involves paying discount points (each point = 1 percent of the loan amount) at closing in exchange for a permanently lower interest rate. A temporary buydown (such as a 2-1 buydown) lowers the rate for the first two years. In 2026, many Tampa Bay builders and some sellers are offering buydowns as incentives to attract buyers. Whether a buydown makes sense depends on how long you plan to keep the loan — typically you need to hold the loan long enough for the interest savings to exceed the upfront cost. Your lender can calculate the break-even point.

Q: How do I get started with the pre-approval process as a Tampa Bay buyer?

Start by gathering your last two years of tax returns, two years of W-2s, 30 days of pay stubs, and two to three months of bank statements for all accounts. Then contact two to three lenders — ideally at least one local lender or credit union and one larger lender — submit your application and documents, and compare the Loan Estimates you receive. Once you have a strong pre-approval letter, contact a local buyer's agent to begin your home search. Having the letter before you start touring puts you in a position to move quickly when you find the right home.

Ready to Buy a Home in Tampa Bay? Let's Start With a Conversation.

Barrett Henry is a Broker Associate with REMAX Collective helping buyers navigate the Tampa Bay real estate market from pre-approval through closing. Whether you are buying your first home, relocating from out of state, or moving up to a larger home, Barrett can connect you with trusted local lenders, guide you through competitive offer strategy, and make sure your financing is solid before you ever write an offer.

title: Mortgage Rate Lock Guide 2026 | When to Lock Your Rate in Tampa Bay
content:
Mortgage Rate Lock Guide 2026

When to Lock Your Mortgage Rate in Tampa Bay — How Rate Locks Work, What They Cost, and How to Protect Your Rate Through Closing

You found your home, your offer was accepted, and now your lender is asking: do you want to lock your rate? This is one of the most consequential decisions in the mortgage process — and one of the least understood. A rate lock protects you from rising rates between now and closing, but it also has costs, deadlines, and fine print that every Tampa Bay buyer needs to understand. Barrett Henry at REMAX Collective helps buyers navigate every step of this process.

Call or Text Barrett Henry: (813) 733-7907

30–60 Days
Most common rate lock periods for Tampa Bay purchase loans
30–45 Days

0.125–0.25%

~0.25–0.5%

±0.25–0.75%

What Is a Mortgage Rate Lock and Why Does It Matter?

A mortgage rate lock is a lender's commitment to hold a specific interest rate for you for a defined period of time, regardless of what happens to rates in the market during that window. Once you lock, your rate is guaranteed — even if rates rise by 0.5% the next week. The lock gives you certainty about your monthly payment and total loan cost, which is critical for budgeting your purchase.

Without a rate lock, you are floating — meaning your rate will be determined by whatever the market is doing on the day your loan closes. This introduces risk in both directions: rates could go down (beneficial), or they could go up (costly). In an environment where rates are volatile or trending upward, most buyers prefer the certainty of a lock over the gamble of floating.

Rate locks are specific. They lock in a rate, a loan program (e.g., 30-year fixed conventional), a loan amount, and a property address. If any of these change materially — you switch from a 30-year to a 15-year, or you buy a different property — the lock may not transfer and you may need to re-lock at current market rates.

Rate Lock Periods: 30, 45, 60, and 90 Days

Lenders offer rate locks in standard periods — most commonly 30, 45, 60, and 90 days. The period you choose should be driven by your anticipated closing timeline, with enough cushion to absorb common delays. Here is how each option works in the Tampa Bay market.

30-day locks are the shortest and most affordable option, often available at no additional cost or at the best available pricing. They work well for purchases where the title search is straightforward, the home inspection has been completed, and the appraisal can be ordered immediately. In Tampa Bay, the average contract-to-close timeline runs 30 to 45 days, so a 30-day lock carries some risk of expiring if anything slows down. Use a 30-day lock only if you have high confidence in your closing timeline.

45-day locks are the most commonly recommended option for typical Tampa Bay resale purchases. They provide just enough buffer for the standard inspection, appraisal, and underwriting cycle while keeping the rate cost reasonable. Many lenders offer 45-day locks at no additional charge or with only a minimal pricing adjustment.

60-day locks are appropriate when there are known complicating factors: a complex title situation, a delayed appraisal market, a self-employed borrower whose file requires extra underwriting time, or a transaction where the seller needs more time to move out. Expect to pay slightly more for a 60-day lock — typically a small adjustment to your rate or in the form of an upfront fee.

90-day locks are most commonly used for new construction purchases where the builder's completion date may be uncertain. They come at a meaningful cost premium — often 0.25% to 0.5% added to your rate, or an equivalent upfront fee. Some lenders offer extended lock programs specifically designed for new construction with periodic extension options.

Float-Down Options: Locking Your Rate But Keeping Upside

One of the most frequently asked questions from Tampa Bay buyers is: what if I lock my rate and then rates drop? The answer may be a float-down option.

A float-down option is an add-on to your rate lock that gives you the right — but not the obligation — to lower your locked rate if market rates fall by a specified amount before closing. You get the security of a locked rate (protection from rising rates) while retaining the ability to benefit if rates move lower.

Float-down options typically come with two conditions: a minimum rate drop threshold that must be met before you can invoke the float-down (often 0.25% or more), and an additional cost paid upfront or built into your rate. Expect to pay approximately 0.25% to 0.5% of the loan amount, or accept a slightly higher locked rate, in exchange for the float-down privilege.

Whether a float-down is worth it depends on the rate environment at the time of your purchase. If rates are near recent highs and market consensus is that they may fall, a float-down option provides meaningful value. If rates are stable or trending upward, the float-down premium may not be worth paying. Discuss current market conditions with your lender and ask whether a float-down makes sense for your specific transaction.

When to Lock Your Rate: Purchase vs. Refinance Timing

For a purchase transaction, the most common practice is to lock your rate at the time of loan application — which is typically shortly after you go under contract. At this point, you know the property, the purchase price, and your loan amount, and you can calculate exactly how long your closing is expected to take. Most Tampa Bay buyer agents recommend locking as soon as your application is submitted, rather than floating and hoping rates drop. Rate certainty removes a major financial variable from an already complex transaction.

For a refinance, timing is more flexible because there is no external deadline imposed by a purchase contract. Refinance borrowers can float their rate while gathering documentation, watch the market, and lock when rates reach a target level. However, floating indefinitely is a gamble — rate movements are notoriously difficult to predict, and waiting for the perfect moment can result in locking at a higher rate than you would have gotten by locking earlier. Most refinance professionals recommend locking when you have reached a rate that meets your financial goals, rather than trying to time the market perfectly.

What Happens If Your Closing Is Delayed Past Your Lock Expiration?

Rate lock expiration is one of the most stressful situations that can arise in a real estate transaction — and it happens more often than buyers expect. Appraisals take longer than anticipated, title issues surface, lender underwriting backlogs develop, or a seller requests a delayed closing. If any of these push your closing date past your lock expiration, you have several options.

Lock extension: The most common solution. Your lender extends the lock period for an additional cost — typically 0.125% to 0.25% of the loan amount per week of extension. On a $400,000 loan, a one-week extension at 0.25% would cost $1,000. Extensions can usually be granted for up to 30 additional days beyond the original lock period, after which a full re-lock at current market rates may be required.

Re-locking at current market rates: If rates have fallen since you originally locked, a market re-lock may actually benefit you. If rates have risen, you are in a difficult position — you will need to lock at the new, higher rate or renegotiate your purchase (in some cases, buyers and sellers split the cost of a rate increase through a price reduction or seller credit).

Who pays for the extension? In most cases, the buyer bears the cost of a lock extension. However, if the delay was caused by the seller (failure to provide required documents, delays in vacating, title issues that were the seller's responsibility), you may have grounds to negotiate a seller credit to cover extension costs. Your real estate agent can help you navigate this conversation.

Rate Lock Extension Costs and Strategies

Understanding the cost structure of rate lock extensions before they happen puts you in a much better negotiating position. Here is what you need to know.

Standard extension fees run 0.125% to 0.25% of the loan amount per week, or sometimes per 5-day or 7-day period. Some lenders structure this as a fee paid at closing, while others require it upfront. Always ask your lender at the time of locking: what are your extension fees, how are they calculated, and how long can you extend before I need a full re-lock?

If your transaction is moving toward the lock expiration date, communicate with your lender early — do not wait until the lock has expired. Many lenders can begin the extension process one to two weeks before expiration. Waiting until the last minute narrows your options and can leave you in a gap period where your rate is neither locked nor floating in a favorable position.

Some lenders offer "worst-case" rate lock programs where they guarantee a rate for up to 90 days with a built-in float-down if rates improve. These can be particularly valuable in uncertain rate environments and are worth asking about if your transaction has a long or uncertain timeline.

Rate Lock Tips Every Tampa Bay Buyer Should Know
  • Always get your rate lock confirmation in writing — a lock email or lock confirmation document from your lender that shows the rate, points, lock period, and expiration date. Do not rely on a verbal confirmation.
  • Understand the lock expiration date and build backwards from it. Know what milestones must be completed (inspection, appraisal, clear to close) and by when. Share this timeline with your real estate agent so everyone is working toward the same deadline.
  • If your lender offers to lock your rate for free for 60 days but charges for 45, it may be worth taking the 60-day lock — the "free" longer lock is often priced into your rate and you are paying for it either way. Ask your lender to show you the pricing comparison.
  • Do not confuse rate lock with rate commitment. A rate lock is not a loan approval — it just holds the rate. Your loan still needs to pass underwriting and satisfy all conditions.
  • New construction buyers: ask your builder about their preferred lender's lock programs. Some builders offer extended lock programs through their preferred lender as an incentive — compare the overall package (rate, fees, credits) to outside lender options.
  • In Tampa Bay, appraisal turnaround times and title search complexity can vary by county and neighborhood. Ask your lender and agent about current turnaround times in your specific area before choosing your lock period.

Mortgage Rate Lock FAQs

Q: What is a mortgage rate lock and how does it work?

A rate lock is a lender's binding commitment to hold a specific interest rate for you for a defined period — typically 30 to 90 days. Once locked, your rate does not change even if market rates rise. The lock is specific to your loan program, loan amount, and property. It expires on a set date, and if your loan does not close by then, you may need to extend or re-lock at current market rates.

Q: When should I lock my mortgage rate in Tampa Bay?

For a purchase, most buyers lock at the time of loan application — shortly after going under contract. This gives you rate certainty while your transaction is in progress. The Tampa Bay contract-to-close timeline typically runs 30 to 45 days, so a 45-day lock usually provides adequate coverage. Lock sooner rather than later if you believe rates may rise, and consult with your lender about current market conditions before deciding.

Q: How much does a rate lock cost?

Standard 30 to 45-day locks are often offered at no additional cost — the pricing is built into the rate you are quoted. Longer locks (60, 90 days) typically carry a cost of 0.125% to 0.5% of the loan amount, either as an upfront fee or as a slightly higher rate. Float-down options add another 0.25% to 0.5%. Always ask your lender to break down what the lock period is adding to your rate or fees so you can compare options accurately.

Q: What is a float-down option and is it worth it?

A float-down lets you lock your rate for protection against increases while retaining the ability to capture a lower rate if the market drops by a specified threshold before closing. It costs approximately 0.25% to 0.5% of the loan amount. Whether it is worth it depends on the rate environment: if rates are volatile and have room to fall, a float-down offers meaningful value. If rates are stable or rising, the premium may not pay off. Ask your lender to model both scenarios.

Q: What happens if my rate lock expires before closing?

You have two main options: extend the lock (typically at 0.125%–0.25% per week) or re-lock at current market rates. If rates have fallen, a re-lock may actually benefit you. If rates have risen, the extension is usually preferable to re-locking at a higher rate. Always communicate with your lender early — do not wait until the lock has already expired. Your agent may also be able to negotiate a seller credit if the delay was seller-caused.

Q: Can I change lenders after I have locked my rate?

Yes, but you will lose your locked rate. Rate locks are lender-specific — they do not transfer. If you switch lenders after locking, you will need to start the process over and lock a new rate at current market conditions with the new lender. It rarely makes sense to change lenders after locking unless you discover a serious issue with the lender's service or fees. This is why it is important to shop lenders and choose carefully before locking.

Q: How long does it take to close on a home in Tampa Bay?

The average Tampa Bay purchase contract closes in 30 to 45 days from the executed contract date, assuming a conventional or FHA loan with a standard file. VA and USDA loans can sometimes take 45 to 60 days due to additional agency requirements. New construction timelines vary significantly based on builder schedule. Your closing date is agreed upon in your purchase contract, and your rate lock period should be chosen to comfortably cover that date with buffer.

Q: Should I float or lock my rate if I think rates are going to drop?

This is a question every buyer asks, and the honest answer is that no one — not lenders, not economists, not financial media — reliably predicts short-term rate movements. The risk of floating is that rates rise instead of fall, costing you significantly more every month for the life of the loan. Most real estate and mortgage professionals recommend locking when you reach a rate that works for your budget. If you want upside exposure, ask about a float-down option rather than floating entirely.

Q: What is the difference between a rate lock for a new construction home vs. a resale home?

New construction rate locks are more complex because the closing timeline is tied to the builder's construction schedule, which can shift by weeks or months. Standard rate locks (30–90 days) may not be sufficient. Many builders offer extended lock programs through their preferred lender — sometimes up to 12 months — with built-in extensions as needed. Always compare the builder's preferred lender package (including rate, fees, and incentives) against outside lenders before committing.

Q: Can I unlock and re-lock if rates drop after I have already locked?

Most standard rate locks do not allow free re-locking — once you lock, you are committed to that rate unless your loan program includes a float-down option. Some lenders offer a one-time re-lock privilege if rates drop by a certain amount, but this is not standard. If you did not purchase a float-down option and rates drop significantly after your lock, you would need to pay extension fees and potentially re-lock at closing, or absorb the fact that you locked at a higher rate. This is another reason float-down options are worth considering in volatile markets.

Have Questions About Locking Your Rate on a Tampa Bay Home Purchase?

Mortgage rate decisions are among the most financially significant choices you will make in the home-buying process. Barrett Henry at REMAX Collective works alongside trusted local lenders to make sure Tampa Bay buyers are informed, protected, and positioned to close on time.

title: Conventional Loan Guide Tampa Bay FL 2026 | Home Financing Explained
content:
Talk to a Tampa Bay ExpertSearch All Homes

Conventional Loan Guide Tampa Bay FL 2026

Conventional loans are the most flexible mortgage option for Tampa Bay buyers with solid credit — offering lower long-term costs, no permanent mortgage insurance, and higher loan limits. Barrett Henry at REMAX Collective will help you use conventional financing to win in today's market.

(813) 733-7907 — Talk to Barrett Henry Today

3%
Minimum Down — HomeReady / Home Possible
$806,500
2026 Conforming Loan Limit
620
Typical Minimum Credit Score
78%
LTV at Which PMI Auto-Cancels
20%
Down Payment to Avoid PMI Entirely
50%
Maximum DTI (with compensating factors)
25%
Min Down — Non-Owner Investment Property
740+
Credit Score for Best Rate Tiers

Conventional loans — those backed by Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac but not insured by a government agency — are the most widely used mortgage product in the United States and the dominant loan type in Tampa Bay's mid-to-upper price range. Unlike FHA, VA, or USDA loans, conventional loans are not government-backed, which means they carry stricter credit and income requirements but also offer significant advantages for qualified buyers.

For Tampa Bay buyers in 2026, conventional financing is particularly powerful because the conforming loan limit of $806,500 covers the vast majority of home purchases throughout Hillsborough, Pinellas, and Pasco counties. Whether you are buying a townhome in South Tampa, a pool home in Westchase, or a waterfront property in Safety Harbor, conventional financing likely applies to your purchase.

The key advantages of conventional loans over government programs include: cancellable PMI (unlike FHA MIP), flexible property type eligibility including non-warrantable condos in some cases, investment property financing, and better pricing for buyers with strong credit scores. For a buyer with 740+ credit, a conventional loan often carries a lower overall cost of borrowing than an FHA loan even when the initial rate looks similar.

Barrett Henry at REMAX Collective works with buyers at every price point across Tampa Bay and helps clients evaluate conventional financing alongside FHA, VA, and USDA options to identify the loan structure that minimizes long-term cost and maximizes purchasing power. The right loan matters as much as the right home.

2026 Conforming Loan Limits

Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac set annual conforming loan limits that define how large a conventional loan can be before it becomes a "jumbo" loan requiring different underwriting. For 2026, the baseline conforming limit is $806,500 for a single-family home — an increase from 2025 that reflects continued home price appreciation nationally.

Units 2026 Conforming Limit Loan Type Above Limit
1-Unit (SFR) $806,500 Jumbo Loan Required
2-Unit $1,032,650 Jumbo Loan Required
3-Unit $1,248,150 Jumbo Loan Required
4-Unit $1,551,250 Jumbo Loan Required

Tampa Bay is not a high-cost area, so the conforming limit equals the national baseline of $806,500. For buyers purchasing above this amount, jumbo loan programs with different down payment and credit requirements apply. Barrett Henry regularly works with buyers in both conventional conforming and jumbo price ranges.

Low Down Payment Options — HomeReady and Home Possible

Many buyers assume you need 20% down for a conventional loan — that is a myth. Fannie Mae's HomeReady and Freddie Mac's Home Possible programs allow down payments as low as 3% with income-based eligibility requirements and reduced mortgage insurance rates.

Fannie Mae HomeReady

HomeReady allows 3% down for buyers at or below 80% of area median income. Boarder income and rental income from accessory dwelling units can be counted toward qualification. Reduced PMI rates apply, and a homebuyer education course is required. Non-borrower household income can be considered to help qualify.

Freddie Mac Home Possible

Home Possible similarly allows 3% down and targets buyers at or below 80% of AMI. It permits co-borrowers who do not occupy the property (useful for parent co-signers), allows sweat equity toward the down payment, and has reduced MI premiums. In Tampa Bay, where AMI limits are relatively accessible for entry-level buyers, both programs are viable tools.

Buyer Tip: The 3% down conventional option requires a minimum 620 credit score, but buyers with 720+ scores will see meaningfully lower PMI rates. If your score is between 620 and 680, run a side-by-side comparison of 3% down conventional vs. 3.5% down FHA — the FHA loan may have a lower total monthly payment even with MIP, depending on your exact scenario.

Private Mortgage Insurance (PMI) — How It Works and How to Eliminate It

When you put less than 20% down on a conventional loan, your lender requires private mortgage insurance to protect against default risk. Unlike FHA MIP, conventional PMI is cancellable — a major advantage for buyers who cannot put 20% down immediately but plan to build equity over time.

PMI Cost Ranges

PMI rates vary based on your credit score, loan-to-value ratio, property type, and PMI provider. Typical annual rates range from 0.2% to 2.0% of the loan amount, with most buyers in the 0.3% to 1.5% range. On a $400,000 loan with 10% down and a 720 credit score, PMI might cost $80–$120 per month.

Credit Score 5% Down PMI Rate 10% Down PMI Rate 15% Down PMI Rate
760+ ~0.30% ~0.20% ~0.15%
720–759 ~0.60% ~0.40% ~0.30%
680–719 ~1.00% ~0.75% ~0.55%
640–679 ~1.40% ~1.10% ~0.80%
620–639 ~1.80% ~1.50% ~1.10%

How to Cancel PMI

Under the Homeowners Protection Act, lenders must automatically cancel PMI when your loan balance reaches 78% of the original purchase price, assuming you are current on payments. You can also request cancellation at 80% LTV with a good payment history. If your home has appreciated significantly, you may be able to order a new appraisal and request PMI cancellation earlier based on current value — a powerful strategy in Tampa Bay's appreciating market.

Credit Score Tiers and Rate Impact

Your credit score is the single most impactful factor in determining your conventional mortgage interest rate. Lenders use Fannie Mae's Loan Level Price Adjustment (LLPA) matrix, which assigns pricing add-ons or discounts based on credit score and LTV. Even a 20-point improvement in your credit score can meaningfully lower your rate.

Credit Score Range LLPA Impact Typical Rate Outcome
760 and above No pricing hit Best available rate
740–759 Minimal adjustment Near-best rate
720–739 Small hit Slightly above best
680–719 Moderate hit Noticeable rate increase
640–679 Significant hit Higher rate; FHA may compete
620–639 Heavy hit FHA often better option

If your score is below 700, it is worth spending a few months improving it before applying. Paying down revolving credit balances, disputing errors, and becoming an authorized user on a family member's established account can each move your score meaningfully. Even a 30-point improvement from 690 to 720 can save thousands over the life of a Tampa Bay mortgage.

Debt-to-Income Ratio Guidelines

Your debt-to-income ratio (DTI) measures monthly debt payments as a percentage of gross monthly income. Conventional loans require both a front-end DTI (housing payment only) and back-end DTI (all monthly debts including the new payment). The standard back-end DTI limit is 43%, but Fannie Mae's Desktop Underwriter (DU) can approve DTIs up to 50% for borrowers with compensating factors such as high credit scores, significant reserves, or low LTV.

  • Standard Maximum DTI: 43% back-end
  • DU Exception Maximum: Up to 50% with strong compensating factors
  • Student Loans: IBR payments count in DTI; zero-payment loans use 0.5–1% of balance
  • Rental Income: Can offset housing payment if properly documented
  • Co-Borrower Income: All borrowers on the loan contribute income and debts

Conventional vs. FHA vs. VA vs. USDA — Complete Comparison

Feature Conventional FHA VA USDA
Min Down Payment 3% 3.5% 0% 0%
Min Credit Score 620 500 No official min 640 typical
Mortgage Insurance PMI (cancellable) MIP (life of loan) VA Funding Fee Guarantee Fee
Eligibility Open to all Open to all Veterans/military Rural areas only
2026 Loan Limit $806,500 $498,257 No limit Varies
Investment Property Yes (20–25% down) No No No
Condo Flexibility Higher (warrantability) Lower (HUD approval) Moderate N/A
Seller Concessions 3–9% (LTV-based) Up to 6% Up to 4% Up to 6%

Investment Property and Second Home Rules

One of conventional financing's unique advantages is that it can be used for investment properties and second homes — something FHA, VA, and USDA loans cannot do. This makes conventional the default loan type for Tampa Bay's thriving real estate investment community.

Investment Property Requirements

  • Minimum 15–20% down for single-unit investment properties (most lenders require 20%)
  • Minimum 25% down for 2-4 unit investment properties
  • 620 minimum credit score, though 680+ is often required by lenders
  • Six months of PITI reserves for each investment property
  • Rental income can be used to qualify (75% of market rent per appraisal)
  • Maximum 10 financed properties per borrower (Fannie Mae guidelines)

Second Home Requirements

  • Minimum 10% down payment
  • Must be occupied by the borrower for some portion of the year
  • Must be suitable for year-round occupancy
  • Cannot be subject to rental agreements or managed by a property management company
  • Must be a reasonable distance from primary residence

Condo Warrantability in Tampa Bay

For conventional loans on condominiums, Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac require the project to be "warrantable" — meeting specific requirements around owner-occupancy, HOA finances, litigation, and building condition. This is less restrictive than FHA condo approval but still requires due diligence, especially post-Surfside in Florida where condo association financial and structural requirements have tightened significantly.

Key warrantability requirements include: no active or pending litigation against the HOA, owner-occupancy rate above 50%, no single entity owning more than 10% of units, adequate reserve funding, and no structural or safety deficiencies. Florida's new condo legislation (SB 4D and follow-on bills) has increased scrutiny of older buildings, particularly those over 30 years old requiring milestone inspections.

Tampa Bay Condo Buyers: Always verify warrantability before falling in love with a condo unit. Some buildings in St. Petersburg, Clearwater Beach, and downtown Tampa have had warrantability issues due to special assessments, litigation, or reserve funding shortfalls. Barrett Henry can help you check project status early in the process to avoid wasted time and inspection costs.

Conventional Loans in Tampa Bay's Competitive Market

In Tampa Bay's competitive market, conventional offers are generally viewed more favorably than FHA offers by listing agents and sellers. This is primarily because conventional appraisals have fewer mandatory property condition requirements, reducing the risk of appraisal-related delays or required repairs.

In multiple-offer situations — which remain common in desirable Tampa Bay communities including South Tampa, Westchase, Odessa, Land O' Lakes, and Dunedin — a conventional pre-approval letter from a local lender can meaningfully strengthen your offer. Barrett Henry advises buyers on how to structure offers that compete effectively while protecting their interests through appropriate contingencies.

Seller concessions on conventional loans are capped at 3% of purchase price for loans with less than 10% down, 6% for 10–25% down, and 9% for 25%+ down. Understanding these caps allows Barrett Henry to structure offers that maximize seller contributions within guidelines.

Step-by-Step Conventional Loan Process

  1. Review Your Credit Profile — Check all three bureau scores, verify no errors, and calculate your DTI to understand where you stand.
  2. Get Pre-Approved — Work with a lender to establish your purchase price ceiling, lock in program type (HomeReady, Home Possible, or standard conventional), and receive your pre-approval letter.
  3. Define Your Home Search — Barrett Henry will identify properties matching your price, location, and property type needs, with attention to condo warrantability where applicable.
  4. Make an Offer — Structure your offer with appropriate financing contingency, seller concession request, and inspection contingency based on market conditions.
  5. Conventional Appraisal — Lender-ordered appraisal focuses on market value; property condition requirements are less strict than FHA.
  6. Underwriting — Lender reviews all documentation; you may receive conditions requesting updated pay stubs, bank statements, or letters of explanation.
  7. Clear to Close — Final approval issued, closing disclosure reviewed, and closing date confirmed.
  8. Close and Move In — Sign documents, wire closing funds, and collect your keys.

Conventional Loan FAQ — Tampa Bay Buyers

What is the minimum down payment for a conventional loan in 2026?

The minimum is 3% for Fannie Mae HomeReady and Freddie Mac Home Possible programs for income-qualifying buyers. Standard conventional loans typically require 5% down. However, putting down at least 20% eliminates PMI entirely and provides the lowest monthly payment and best rate pricing. Most Tampa Bay buyers choose somewhere between 5% and 20% depending on their cash position and timeline.

What is the 2026 conventional conforming loan limit for Tampa Bay?

The 2026 conforming loan limit for a single-family home in Hillsborough, Pinellas, and Pasco counties is $806,500. Loans above this amount are considered jumbo loans and require separate underwriting with typically higher down payments and credit requirements. The conforming limit covers most homes in Tampa Bay, making conventional the dominant loan type across the market.

Can I cancel PMI on a conventional loan?

Yes — and this is one of conventional's major advantages over FHA. You can request PMI cancellation at 80% LTV with a good payment history. Lenders must automatically cancel PMI when your balance reaches 78% of the original purchase price. If your home has appreciated, you may be able to request cancellation earlier based on current appraised value. This is a common strategy for Tampa Bay buyers who purchased in appreciating neighborhoods.

What credit score do I need for the best conventional mortgage rate?

A score of 760 or higher typically qualifies for the best conventional rate pricing with no LLPA surcharges. Scores between 740 and 759 receive near-best pricing. Below 720, pricing adjustments become more noticeable. Buyers with scores below 680 may find FHA loans competitive on a total cost basis, especially for lower down payments. Even modest credit improvements before application can save thousands of dollars over a 30-year mortgage.

Can I use a conventional loan to buy an investment property in Tampa Bay?

Yes. Conventional is the primary financing vehicle for investment properties. Single-unit investment properties require 15–20% down (most lenders require 20%), while 2-4 unit investment properties require 25% down. You need a credit score of at least 620, though many lenders require 680+. Rental income can be used to offset the new payment in your DTI calculation. Barrett Henry works with investors throughout Tampa Bay and can connect you with investment-property-experienced lenders.

What is the maximum DTI for a conventional loan?

The standard guideline is 43% back-end DTI, but Fannie Mae's automated underwriting system (DU) can approve DTIs up to 45–50% for borrowers with compensating factors including high credit scores (720+), significant cash reserves (12+ months of housing payments), and low LTV ratios. If your DTI is above 43%, getting pre-approved through DU before searching for homes will clarify exactly where you stand.

Is a conventional loan better than FHA for Tampa Bay buyers?

It depends on your credit score, down payment, and long-term plans. Buyers with 720+ credit and at least 5% down often benefit from conventional due to lower total MI costs and cancellable PMI. Buyers with 580–680 credit or limited down payment may benefit from FHA's more lenient pricing and higher seller concession limits. The best approach is to get quotes for both programs and compare total monthly payment, upfront costs, and long-term MI expense — something Barrett Henry and an experienced lender can help you model.

What makes a condo "warrantable" for a conventional loan?

A warrantable condo meets Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac guidelines including: over 50% owner-occupancy, no single entity owning more than 10% of units, no active litigation involving the HOA, adequate reserve funding (10%+ of HOA budget), no commercial space exceeding 35% of the building, and no structural or safety issues. Non-warrantable condos require portfolio or non-QM financing, which carries higher rates and stricter terms.

How do seller concessions work on a conventional loan?

Conventional seller concession limits vary by down payment: up to 3% for less than 10% down, up to 6% for 10–24.99% down, and up to 9% for 25%+ down. Concessions can cover origination fees, title costs, prepaid taxes and insurance, discount points, and other allowable closing costs. Barrett Henry negotiates seller concessions as part of every offer where appropriate, particularly in neighborhoods with more seller flexibility.

What are Fannie Mae's HomeReady and Freddie Mac's Home Possible programs?

HomeReady (Fannie Mae) and Home Possible (Freddie Mac) are low down payment conventional programs designed for income-qualified buyers at or below 80% of area median income. Both allow 3% down with reduced PMI rates, and both permit non-traditional income sources including boarder income. A homebuyer education course is required. In Tampa Bay, these programs are ideal for first-time buyers who want a conventional loan but cannot yet put 5% or more down.

Ready to Buy in Tampa Bay with Conventional Financing?

Barrett Henry at REMAX Collective helps Tampa Bay buyers evaluate conventional, FHA, VA, and USDA options side by side — and negotiate offers that win. Get expert buyer representation from pre-approval to closing.

(813) 733-7907 — Call Barrett Henry

  • VA Loan Guide Tampa Bay

Buyer Resources

  • Home Inspection Guide
  • First-Time Buyer Guide
  • Closing Costs Guide

title: FHA Loan Guide Tampa Bay FL 2026 | Low Down Payment Home Loans
content:
Talk to a Tampa Bay ExpertSearch All Homes

FHA Loan Guide Tampa Bay FL 2026

Buy your Tampa Bay home with as little as 3.5% down. FHA loans are one of the most flexible mortgage programs available — and Barrett Henry at REMAX Collective will help you use them to your advantage.

(813) 733-7907 — Talk to Barrett Henry Today

3.5%
Minimum Down Payment (580+ Credit)
$498,257
2026 FHA Limit — Hillsborough County
580
Minimum Credit Score for 3.5% Down
1.75%
Upfront MIP (Mortgage Insurance Premium)
6%
Maximum Seller Concessions Allowed
0.55%
Annual MIP — Low LTV/Strong Credit Scenarios
43%
Standard Max DTI (up to 57% with compensating factors)
500
Minimum Credit Score (10% Down Required)

FHA loans — backed by the Federal Housing Administration — have helped millions of Americans become homeowners, and they remain one of the most powerful tools for buyers in the Tampa Bay market. Whether you are a first-time buyer in Brandon, upsizing in Wesley Chapel, or purchasing a condo in St. Petersburg, understanding how FHA financing works will give you a clear edge in your home search.

The core appeal of an FHA loan is the low barrier to entry. Buyers with a credit score of 580 or higher can purchase a home with just 3.5% down. That means on a $350,000 home in Riverview, your minimum down payment is $12,250 — far below the $70,000 required for a conventional 20% down scenario. For buyers with credit scores between 500 and 579, FHA still offers a path to homeownership, but requires 10% down.

FHA loans come with mortgage insurance requirements that conventional loans can avoid with 20% down — but they also offer more lenient qualification standards, higher allowable debt-to-income ratios, and flexible gift fund rules. For many Tampa Bay buyers, the trade-off is well worth it, particularly in a market where home prices have appreciated significantly over the past several years.

Barrett Henry at REMAX Collective works with buyers throughout Hillsborough, Pinellas, and Pasco counties and can help you evaluate whether FHA is the right fit for your situation, identify sellers willing to offer concessions, and navigate the FHA appraisal and approval process from start to close.

2026 FHA Loan Limits — Tampa Bay Counties

FHA loan limits are set annually by HUD and vary by county. For 2026, Tampa Bay area limits are as follows:

County 1-Unit Limit 2-Unit Limit 3-Unit Limit 4-Unit Limit
Hillsborough $498,257 $637,950 $771,125 $958,350
Pinellas $498,257 $637,950 $771,125 $958,350
Pasco $498,257 $637,950 $771,125 $958,350

These limits apply to the total loan amount — not the purchase price. Because Tampa Bay median home prices in many suburbs fall well within these limits, FHA financing is a viable option across a wide range of neighborhoods including Riverview, Brandon, Wesley Chapel, New Port Richey, Largo, and Clearwater.

FHA Mortgage Insurance Premiums (MIP) Explained

FHA loans require two forms of mortgage insurance: an upfront premium and an annual premium. Understanding both is essential for accurately calculating your monthly payment.

Upfront MIP (UFMIP)

The upfront mortgage insurance premium is 1.75% of the base loan amount, paid at closing. It can be rolled into the loan rather than paid out of pocket. On a $400,000 loan, UFMIP equals $7,000 — which most borrowers finance into the loan balance.

Annual MIP

The annual MIP is charged monthly as part of your payment and ranges from 0.55% to 1.05% of the loan amount depending on your loan term, loan-to-value ratio, and loan size. For most 30-year FHA loans with less than 10% down, the annual MIP is 0.55%.

Loan Term LTV Ratio Annual MIP Rate Duration
30-year ≤ 90% LTV 0.50% 11 years
30-year > 90% LTV 0.55% Loan life
15-year ≤ 90% LTV 0.15% 11 years
15-year > 90% LTV 0.40% Loan life
Important: Unlike conventional loans, FHA MIP on a 30-year loan with less than 10% down lasts for the life of the loan. To eliminate MIP, borrowers typically refinance into a conventional loan once they have 20% equity. This is a common strategy for Tampa Bay buyers who plan to stay in their home several years and expect appreciation to build equity faster.

FHA vs. Conventional — Side-by-Side Comparison

Feature FHA Loan Conventional Loan
Minimum Down Payment 3.5% (580+ credit) 3% (HomeReady/Home Possible)
Minimum Credit Score 500 (with 10% down) 620 typically
Mortgage Insurance Required (UFMIP + annual MIP) PMI required below 20% down
MI Cancellation Must refinance (if <10% down) Automatic at 78% LTV
Loan Limit (Hillsborough) $498,257 $806,500 (conforming)
DTI Maximum 43–57% (with AUS approval) 43–50% (varies by lender)
Seller Concessions Up to 6% 3–9% (varies by LTV)
Property Condition Stricter (MPR appraisal) More flexible
Gift Funds for Down Payment 100% allowed Allowed with restrictions

FHA-Approved Condos in Tampa Bay

Using an FHA loan to purchase a condominium adds an extra layer of complexity unique to this loan type. Unlike conventional loans, FHA requires the entire condominium project — not just the individual unit — to be on HUD's approved condo list. This is a critical consideration in Tampa Bay, where many buyers target condos in St. Petersburg, Clearwater Beach, South Tampa, and Ybor City.

To be FHA-approved, a condo project must meet requirements including: at least 50% owner-occupancy rate, no more than 15% of units delinquent on HOA dues, adequate reserve funding, no single entity owning more than 10% of units, and proper insurance coverage. Many waterfront and luxury condo buildings in Tampa Bay do not meet these criteria, which limits FHA buyers to specific approved projects.

The good news is that FHA's Single-Unit Approval process allows buyers to purchase in non-approved projects that meet eligibility criteria. Barrett Henry can help you navigate both approved project lists and the spot approval process for specific units you are interested in.

FHA Minimum Property Requirements (MPR)

FHA appraisals evaluate both the market value of a property and its condition. Properties must meet HUD's Minimum Property Requirements to qualify for FHA financing. In Florida's hot and humid climate, these requirements carry special significance.

Common FHA appraisal issues in Tampa Bay include: roof condition and remaining useful life (FHA typically requires at least 2 years of remaining life), evidence of standing water or drainage problems in flood-prone areas, peeling paint on pre-1978 homes (lead paint protocol), non-functional HVAC systems, active pest infestations, and health/safety hazards such as exposed wiring or missing handrails.

Buyer Tip: If you are making an FHA offer on an older home or a property that has been sitting vacant, order a pre-inspection before your FHA appraisal. Knowing about condition issues in advance lets you negotiate repairs with the seller or adjust your offer price — rather than learning about them mid-transaction when timelines are tight.

Seller Concessions and Closing Cost Strategy

FHA allows sellers to contribute up to 6% of the purchase price toward the buyer's closing costs. This is significantly more generous than conventional loans, which cap seller contributions at 3% for buyers putting less than 10% down. In Tampa Bay's real estate market, this creates real opportunity — especially for buyers who are tight on cash after making a down payment.

Seller concessions can cover loan origination fees, title insurance, prepaid taxes and insurance, escrow deposits, discount points, and other allowable closing costs. On a $350,000 purchase, a 6% seller concession equals $21,000 — enough to cover virtually all closing costs and even buy down the interest rate.

Barrett Henry regularly structures FHA purchase offers with seller concession requests tailored to the competitiveness of each listing. In neighborhoods like Brandon and Riverview where there is more inventory, sellers are often willing to help — especially with FHA buyers who come with strong pre-approval letters and clean offers.

FHA Streamline Refinance

One underappreciated benefit of FHA loans is the FHA Streamline Refinance program. If you already have an FHA loan and rates drop, you can refinance with reduced documentation — no full appraisal required, no income verification in many cases, and a streamlined underwriting process. The primary requirement is that the refinance must provide a "net tangible benefit" — typically a lower monthly payment or reduced interest rate.

For Tampa Bay buyers who purchase with FHA today, a streamline refinance could be a valuable option if interest rates decline in 2027 or 2028. Planning your exit from MIP through either a streamline refinance or a conventional refinance at 20% equity is part of a smart long-term ownership strategy.

Tampa Bay Neighborhoods Where FHA Is Common

FHA financing is particularly prevalent in suburban Tampa Bay communities where home prices align well with FHA loan limits and first-time buyers are active. Here is where Barrett Henry sees FHA used most frequently:

  • Riverview — Fast-growing community with strong new construction and resale inventory under $450,000
  • Brandon — Established suburb with high FHA transaction volume; sellers experienced with concession requests
  • Wesley Chapel (Pasco County) — Master-planned communities with entry-level single-family homes
  • New Port Richey / Zephyrhills — Affordable Pasco County markets where FHA buyers dominate
  • Largo / Pinellas Park — Pinellas County entry-level market with condos and small single-family homes
  • Apollo Beach / Sun City Center — Southern Hillsborough with FHA-eligible price points

HUD-Approved Housing Counselors in Tampa Bay

HUD requires housing counseling for certain FHA borrowers, and recommends it for all first-time buyers. HUD-approved housing counselors provide pre-purchase education, credit coaching, and budgeting guidance — often for free or at very low cost. In Tampa Bay, HUD-approved agencies include:

  • Eckerd Connects — Tampa-based nonprofit with homebuyer education programs
  • Pinellas Community Foundation — Housing counseling resources for Pinellas County buyers
  • Neighborworks America — National network with Florida partners offering free online counseling
  • Florida Housing Finance Corporation — State-level programs including homebuyer education certification

Completing a HUD-approved homebuyer education course is also often required to access down payment assistance programs stacked on top of FHA loans — a powerful combination for buyers with limited cash reserves.

Step-by-Step FHA Purchase Process

  1. Check Your Credit Score — Pull your report from all three bureaus. Address any errors, pay down balances, and avoid new credit inquiries before applying.
  2. Get Pre-Approved by an FHA Lender — Work with a lender who specializes in FHA. They will calculate your maximum loan amount, estimate MIP, and issue a pre-approval letter.
  3. Define Your Home Search — Work with Barrett Henry to identify homes that are FHA-eligible in price, condition, and property type.
  4. Make an Offer — Include an FHA financing contingency and request seller concessions appropriate to the market conditions.
  5. FHA Appraisal — Your lender orders an FHA appraisal that evaluates both value and property condition per HUD's MPR guidelines.
  6. Underwriting — The lender reviews your income, assets, employment, and the appraisal to issue a loan commitment.
  7. Clear to Close — Final conditions are satisfied, closing disclosure is issued, and closing is scheduled.
  8. Closing Day — Sign documents, pay your down payment and any remaining closing costs, and receive your keys.

FHA Loan FAQ — Tampa Bay Buyers

What is the minimum credit score for an FHA loan in 2026?

The FHA minimum credit score is 500, but you need at least 580 to qualify for the 3.5% down payment option. Borrowers with scores between 500 and 579 must put 10% down. Individual lenders may impose higher "overlay" minimums — typically 580 to 620 — so shopping multiple lenders is important if your score is on the lower end.

Can I use gift funds for my FHA down payment?

Yes. FHA loans allow 100% of the down payment and closing costs to come from gift funds provided by family members, employers, labor unions, or charitable organizations. The gift must be documented with a gift letter stating no repayment is required, along with evidence of the transfer. This makes FHA an excellent option for buyers receiving financial help from parents or relatives.

How does FHA mortgage insurance differ from PMI on a conventional loan?

FHA MIP consists of two parts: a 1.75% upfront premium (typically rolled into the loan) and an annual premium of 0.50–0.55% paid monthly. Unlike conventional PMI, FHA MIP on a 30-year loan with less than 10% down cannot be canceled — it lasts for the life of the loan. To eliminate MIP, most borrowers refinance into a conventional loan once they reach 20% equity. Conventional PMI automatically cancels at 78% LTV.

Are FHA loans harder to get accepted on in Tampa Bay's competitive market?

Some sellers and listing agents are cautious about FHA offers because they worry about stricter appraisal requirements and potential property condition issues. However, with a strong pre-approval letter, flexible closing timeline, and experienced representation from Barrett Henry, FHA offers routinely succeed. In high-inventory suburbs like Brandon and Riverview, FHA is commonly accepted. In highly competitive bidding situations, an FHA buyer may benefit from a seller incentive structure that makes the offer more attractive.

What are the FHA loan limits for Tampa Bay in 2026?

For 2026, the FHA loan limit for a single-family home in Hillsborough, Pinellas, and Pasco counties is $498,257. This limit applies to the loan amount, not the purchase price. Multi-unit limits are higher: $637,950 for 2-unit, $771,125 for 3-unit, and $958,350 for 4-unit properties. These limits cover a large portion of the Tampa Bay resale market.

Can I buy a condo with an FHA loan in Tampa Bay?

Yes, but the condo project must be on HUD's approved condo list or qualify for single-unit approval. Not all Tampa Bay condo buildings are FHA-approved — many luxury and waterfront buildings do not meet owner-occupancy and HOA financial requirements. Barrett Henry can check FHA condo approval status for any building you are considering and advise on alternative financing if needed.

How much can a seller contribute to closing costs on an FHA loan?

Sellers can contribute up to 6% of the purchase price toward the FHA buyer's closing costs. This is one of the most generous seller concession limits of any loan program. On a $400,000 purchase, that is up to $24,000 the seller can contribute — covering essentially all closing costs and potentially buying down the interest rate. Barrett Henry routinely negotiates seller concessions for FHA buyers in Tampa Bay.

What is an FHA Streamline Refinance?

An FHA Streamline Refinance allows existing FHA borrowers to refinance quickly with reduced paperwork — typically no full appraisal, no employment verification, and no income verification. The loan must result in a net tangible benefit such as a lower monthly payment. The streamline option is valuable if mortgage rates fall significantly after your purchase, allowing you to lower your payment without a lengthy underwriting process.

Can I use an FHA loan to buy investment property or a vacation home?

No. FHA loans require owner-occupancy — you must intend to use the property as your primary residence within 60 days of closing. You cannot use FHA financing for a pure investment property or a second/vacation home. However, FHA does allow you to purchase a 2-to-4 unit property with one unit serving as your primary residence, which can be a powerful house-hacking strategy for Tampa Bay buyers.

Do I need a HUD-approved housing counselor to get an FHA loan?

HUD counseling is not required for standard FHA purchase loans, but it is required for FHA reverse mortgages and recommended for all first-time buyers. Completing a HUD-approved homebuyer education course often unlocks access to Florida down payment assistance programs and grants that can be layered with FHA financing. Many Tampa Bay buyers find that spending a few hours on a homebuyer education course results in thousands of dollars in additional assistance.

Ready to Buy in Tampa Bay with FHA Financing?
